1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the LORD speaking again to Moses, opening the matter of the workmen. Gill notes that, after the LORD had given Moses instructions about building the tabernacle, the model of which he had shown him, he now speaks of the men to make it. The witnesses note God speaking again to Moses, to name the workmen who should make the tabernacle and its furniture; the turning from the pattern to the workmen, that God provides the men as well as the plan — that the LORD now appoints who shall do the work: "the LORD spake unto Moses, saying"; the witnesses note the connexion, that this followed "after the LORD had given Moses instructions about building" the tabernacle and all its furniture; the witnesses note that, having shown the pattern, God now provides for the doing of it, naming and fitting the men; the witnesses note the order, that God appoints both the work and the workmen, the plan and the hands to execute it; the witnesses note the comfort the commentators draw, that God who calls to a work also fits and provides for it, giving the men and the means; the witnesses note that nothing is wanting where God appoints, for he supplies all; the witnesses note that the same God who designs his house raises up those who shall build it; so that the LORD speaks to provide the workmen; the LORD spake unto Moses, saying. The witnesses note And the LORD spake unto Moses. The witnesses note saying.

The witnesses note the LORD speaking again to Moses, turning from the pattern of the tabernacle to the workmen who should make it — God providing the men as well as the plan; God who calls to a work also fits and provides for it, the same God who designs his house raising up those who shall build it.

So the witnesses make the verse the turning from the pattern to the workmen. The lesson the expositors draw is that God, having shown the plan, now provides the men, fitting and appointing the workmen, God who calls to a work also providing for it. The witnesses note the spake again: "the LORD spake unto Moses" — Gill notes after the instructions; the spake again; the after the instructions; the the pattern; the the tabernacle; the shown. The expositors observe the the workmen: now of the workmen; the the workmen; the the workmen; the the men; the to make it; the named. The witnesses press the plan and hands: the plan and the hands; the plan and hands; the the plan; the the hands; the the work; the to do it. The expositors draw the lesson on the fits for the work: God fits for the work; the fits for the work; the fits; the the work; the provides; the the means. The witnesses observe the nothing wanting: nothing wanting where God appoints; the nothing wanting; the nothing; the wanting; the God appoints; the supplies. The expositors note the raises up builders: God raises up the builders; the raises up builders; the raises up; the builders; the his house; the designs. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And the LORD spake unto Moses, the saying, and the workmen. The verse thus records the turning to the workmen — teaching that God, having shown the plan, now provides the men, God who calls to a work providing for it.
2:
"See, I have called by name Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah:" The witnesses note God calling Bezaleel by name to be the chief workman. Gill notes God had pitched upon, chosen, and distinguished Bezaleel particularly from all other men to be the architect or master-builder of the tabernacle. The witnesses note God declaring he had called Bezaleel by name, the son of Uri, of the tribe of Judah, to be the chief workman; the divine choosing and naming of the master-builder — that God Himself singles out the man for the work: "See, I have called by name Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah"; the witnesses note the calling, "I have called by name Bezaleel" — that God had "pitched upon, chosen, and distinguished him particularly from all other men," calling him by name to the work; the witnesses note the man, "the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah" — of an honourable line (Hur being he who held up Moses' hands, 17:12), and of the royal tribe of Judah; the witnesses note the dignity, that to be "called by name" of God to a service is a high honour, God himself appointing the man; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that God calls his servants by name to their work, as he calls his people, knowing and choosing each particularly; the witnesses note that the chief workman of God's house was of Judah, the tribe of Christ; the witnesses note that God fits and names whom he will for his service; so that God calls Bezaleel by name to the work; of the tribe of Judah. The witnesses note See, I have called by name Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur. The witnesses note of the tribe of Judah.

The witnesses note God calling Bezaleel by name — of the line of Hur and the royal tribe of Judah — singling him out from all others to be the master-builder of the tabernacle; God calling his servants by name to their work, as he calls his people, knowing and choosing each particularly.

So the witnesses make the verse the divine choosing and naming of the master-builder. The lesson the expositors draw is that God called Bezaleel by name, distinguishing him from all others, God calling his servants by name to their work as he calls his people. The witnesses note the called by name: "I have called by name Bezaleel" — Gill notes chosen particularly; the called by name; the chosen particularly; the distinguished; the from all others; the the architect. The expositors observe the the tribe of Judah: "of the tribe of Judah" — the royal tribe; the the tribe of Judah; the the royal tribe; the the son of Hur; the honourable; the the line. The witnesses press the a high honour: called by name a high honour; the a high honour; the high honour; the to a service; the God appointing; the the man. The expositors draw the lesson on the calls by name: God calls his servants by name; the calls by name; the calls; the by name; the his servants; the his people. The expositors note the of Judah: the chief workman of Judah, the tribe of Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the of the tribe of Judah, and the calling. The verse thus records the choosing of the master-builder — teaching that God called Bezaleel by name, God calling his servants by name to their work.
3:
"And I have filled him with the spirit of God, in wisdom, and in understanding, and in knowledge, and in all manner of workmanship," The witnesses note Bezaleel filled with the Spirit of God for the work. Gill notes he was filled, not with the special graces of the Spirit, or with spiritual gifts fitting for spiritual service, but with skill, wisdom, and ability for this work. The witnesses note God declaring he had filled Bezaleel with the Spirit of God, in wisdom, understanding, knowledge, and all workmanship; the gift of the Spirit for skill and ability in the work — that God's Spirit fits men even for the work of the hands: "I have filled him with the spirit of God, in wisdom, and in understanding, and in knowledge, and in all manner of workmanship"; the witnesses note the filling, "filled him with the spirit of God" — that this was not "the special graces of the Spirit, or... spiritual gifts fitting for spiritual service," but the Spirit giving skill and ability for the work; the witnesses note the gifts named, "wisdom... understanding... knowledge... all manner of workmanship" — wisdom to design, understanding to order, knowledge to execute, and skill in every kind of craft; the witnesses note the great lesson the commentators draw, that all ability and skill, even in arts and crafts, is the gift of God's Spirit, who fits men for whatever work he calls them to; the witnesses note that the wisdom of the workman, as of the believer, comes from above; the witnesses note that God qualifies whom he calls, filling them for the work; so that Bezaleel is filled with the Spirit for the work; in all manner of workmanship. The witnesses note I have filled him with the spirit of God, in wisdom, and in understanding, and in knowledge. The witnesses note and in all manner of workmanship.

The witnesses note Bezaleel filled with the Spirit of God in wisdom, understanding, knowledge, and all workmanship — not the special graces of the Spirit, but the Spirit giving skill and ability for the work; all ability and skill, even in arts and crafts, the gift of God's Spirit, who fits men for whatever work he calls them to.

So the witnesses make the verse the gift of the Spirit for skill in the work. The lesson the expositors draw is that God filled Bezaleel with the Spirit in wisdom and skill, all ability the gift of God's Spirit, who qualifies whom he calls. The witnesses note the filled with the Spirit: "filled him with the spirit of God" — Gill notes for the work; the filled with the Spirit; the for the work; the not special graces; the not spiritual gifts; the skill. The expositors observe the wisdom and skill: "wisdom... understanding... knowledge" — to design and execute; the wisdom and skill; the wisdom to design; the understanding to order; the knowledge to execute; the all craft. The witnesses press the all ability from God: all ability from God's Spirit; the all ability from God; the all ability; the from God; the the Spirit; the arts and crafts. The expositors draw the lesson on the wisdom from above: the workman's wisdom from above; the wisdom from above; the wisdom; the from above; the the workman; the the believer. The expositors note the fits for the work: the Spirit fits for the work. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and in all manner of workmanship, and the Spirit. The verse thus records the gift of the Spirit — teaching that God filled Bezaleel with skill, all ability the gift of God's Spirit.
4:
"To devise cunning works, to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass," The witnesses note Bezaleel fitted to devise cunning works in gold, silver, and brass. Gill notes he was enabled to invent, contrive, and draw patterns for the weavers, and for making the curtains and other parts, and to work in the precious metals. The witnesses note God fitting Bezaleel to devise cunning works and to work in gold, silver, and brass; the skill given to design and to work the metals — that the Spirit gives him art to invent and to fashion: "To devise cunning works, to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass"; the witnesses note the devising, "to devise cunning works" — that he was able "to invent, contrive, and draw patterns," to design the curious work; the witnesses note the metals, "to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass" — that he had skill to fashion the three metals of the tabernacle, the gold of the holy things, the silver of the foundation, and the brass of the court; the witnesses note that this skill, both to design and to execute, was the gift of the Spirit; the witnesses note the meaning, that God gives both the inventing mind and the working hand, the device and the doing; the witnesses note that all the curious and beautiful work of God's house was wrought by Spirit-given skill; the witnesses note that the arts that serve God's worship are his own gift; so that Bezaleel devises and works the metals; to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass. The witnesses note To devise cunning works. The witnesses note to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass.

The witnesses note Bezaleel fitted to devise cunning works and to work in gold, silver, and brass — both the inventing mind and the working hand the gift of the Spirit; all the curious and beautiful work of God's house wrought by Spirit-given skill, the arts that serve God's worship his own gift.

So the witnesses make the verse the skill given to design and to work the metals. The lesson the expositors draw is that God gave Bezaleel both to devise and to work, the device and the doing the gift of the Spirit, the arts of God's house his own gift. The witnesses note the devise cunning works: "to devise cunning works" — Gill notes to invent and draw patterns; the devise cunning works; the to invent; the contrive; the draw patterns; the the design. The expositors observe the the metals: "gold... silver... brass" — the three metals; the the metals; the the three metals; the the gold; the the silver; the the brass. The witnesses press the design and execute: both design and execute; the design and execute; the design; the execute; the the skill; the the gift. The expositors draw the lesson on the mind and hand: the inventing mind and working hand; the mind and hand; the the mind; the the hand; the the device; the the doing. The witnesses observe the Spirit-given skill: wrought by Spirit-given skill; the Spirit-given skill; the Spirit-given; the skill; the the work; the the house. The expositors note the arts his gift: the arts of worship his gift; the arts his gift; the the arts; the his gift; the worship; the God's. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the To devise cunning works, the to work in gold and in silver and in brass, and the skill. The verse thus records the skill given — teaching that God gave both to devise and to work, all the work of his house wrought by Spirit-given skill.
5:
"And in cutting of stones, to set them, and in carving of timber, to work in all manner of workmanship." The witnesses note Bezaleel skilled in cutting stones and carving timber, in all workmanship. Gill notes the stones were not common building stones, but precious stones to be cut and set, as those of the ephod and breastplate, and he was skilled also in carving wood. The witnesses note God fitting Bezaleel in cutting and setting stones and carving timber, in all manner of workmanship; the further skill in the precious stones and the wood — that his Spirit-given art reached every craft of the work: "in cutting of stones, to set them, and in carving of timber, to work in all manner of workmanship"; the witnesses note the stones, "cutting of stones, to set them" — not common stones, but "precious stones to be cut and set," as the gems of the ephod and breastplate; the witnesses note the timber, "carving of timber" — skill to carve the shittim wood of the boards and other parts; the witnesses note the breadth, "to work in all manner of workmanship" — that his skill reached every kind of craft needed for the tabernacle, metal, stone, and wood; the witnesses note the meaning, that the Spirit fitted him for the whole work, in all its variety, leaving nothing beyond his given skill; the witnesses note that God supplies all the gifts needed for his service, none lacking; the witnesses note that the whole work of the house was provided for in the one Spirit-filled workman and his fellows; so that Bezaleel is skilled in stone and wood and all craft; to work in all manner of workmanship. The witnesses note And in cutting of stones, to set them, and in carving of timber. The witnesses note to work in all manner of workmanship.

The witnesses note Bezaleel skilled in cutting and setting precious stones (as the gems of the ephod) and carving timber, in all manner of workmanship — the Spirit fitting him for the whole work in all its variety; God supplying all the gifts needed for his service, none lacking, the whole work provided for in the Spirit-filled workman.

So the witnesses make the verse the further skill in stones and wood, in all craft. The lesson the expositors draw is that Bezaleel was fitted for every kind of workmanship, the Spirit supplying all the gifts needed for the work, none lacking. The witnesses note the cutting of stones: "cutting of stones, to set them" — Gill notes precious stones; the cutting of stones; the precious stones; the cut and set; the the ephod; the the breastplate. The expositors observe the carving of timber: "carving of timber" — the wood; the carving of timber; the the wood; the the boards; the the shittim; the carved. The witnesses press the all workmanship: "all manner of workmanship" — every craft; the all workmanship; the every craft; the metal; the stone; the wood. The expositors draw the lesson on the the whole work: fitted for the whole work; the the whole work; the the whole; the the work; the all its variety; the nothing beyond. The witnesses observe the all gifts supplied: God supplies all gifts; the all gifts supplied; the all gifts; the supplied; the his service; the none lacking. The expositors note the provided in the workman: the work provided in the workman; the provided in the workman; the provided; the the workman; the Spirit-filled; the and his fellows. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And in cutting of stones to set them and in carving of timber, the to work in all manner of workmanship, and the skill. The verse thus records the further skill — teaching that Bezaleel was fitted for every craft, God supplying all the gifts needed for his service.
6:
"And I, behold, I have given with him Aholiab, the son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan: and in the hearts of all that are wise hearted I have put wisdom, that they may make all that I have commanded thee;" The witnesses note Aholiab given as Bezaleel's helper, and wisdom put in all the wise-hearted. Gill notes Aholiab, of the tribe of Dan, was given to be a partner with Bezaleel and to assist in the work, and wisdom was put into the hearts of all the wise-hearted. The witnesses note God giving Aholiab to help Bezaleel, and putting wisdom in the hearts of all the wise-hearted, that they might make all he commanded; the appointing of a helper and the gifting of many for the work — that God provides fellow-workers and fills them all with skill: "I have given with him Aholiab... and in the hearts of all that are wise hearted I have put wisdom, that they may make all that I have commanded thee"; the witnesses note the helper, "I have given with him Aholiab... of the tribe of Dan" — a partner and assistant to Bezaleel, of another tribe, that the work might be shared; the witnesses note the many, "in the hearts of all that are wise hearted I have put wisdom" — that God put skill into the hearts of all the able workmen, not Bezaleel and Aholiab only; the witnesses note the end, "that they may make all that I have commanded thee" — that all this skill was given for the doing of God's commanded work, after the pattern; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that God gives variety of gifts and many workers for his work, each fitted in his place, and that even the wisdom of the hands is his putting; the witnesses note that the whole work is carried by many, all gifted of God; so that helpers are given and the wise-hearted filled with skill; that they may make all that I have commanded thee. The witnesses note I have given with him Aholiab, the son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan. The witnesses note and in the hearts of all that are wise hearted I have put wisdom, that they may make all that I have commanded thee.

The witnesses note Aholiab given as Bezaleel's partner and wisdom put in all the wise-hearted, that they might make all God commanded — God providing fellow-workers and filling them all with skill; God gives variety of gifts and many workers for his work, each fitted in his place, even the wisdom of the hands his putting.

So the witnesses make the verse the appointing of a helper and the gifting of many. The lesson the expositors draw is that God gave Aholiab to help and put wisdom in all the wise-hearted, providing many workers, each fitted, all gifted of God. The witnesses note the given Aholiab: "I have given with him Aholiab" — Gill notes a partner; the to assist; the of Dan. The expositors observe the wise hearted: "all that are wise hearted" — the able workmen; the many. The witnesses press the put wisdom: "I have put wisdom" — God's putting; the in their hearts. The expositors draw the lesson on the make all commanded: "make all that I have commanded" — after the pattern; the to do. The expositors note the carried by many: the work carried by many, all gifted of God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and in the hearts of all that are wise hearted I have put wisdom that they may make all that I have commanded thee, and the helpers. The verse thus records the helper and the many — teaching that God gave Aholiab and filled the wise-hearted, providing many workers, all gifted of God.
7:
"The tabernacle of the congregation, and the ark of the testimony, and the mercy seat that is thereupon, and all the furniture of the tabernacle," The witnesses note the tabernacle, ark, and mercy seat among the things the workmen were to make. Gill notes they had wisdom given to make the tabernacle according to the pattern showed to Moses, and under the direction of Bezaleel and Aholiab, to whom Moses communicated it. The witnesses note God naming the tabernacle, the ark of the testimony, the mercy seat, and all the furniture, among the things to be made; the beginning of the list of holy things the gifted workmen should make — that all the holy things are to be wrought after the pattern: "The tabernacle of the congregation, and the ark of the testimony, and the mercy seat that is thereupon, and all the furniture"; the witnesses note the order, that "from henceforward... mention" is made of all the things to be made by the Spirit-filled workmen, beginning with the chief; the witnesses note the tabernacle itself, "the tabernacle of the congregation," God's dwelling; the witnesses note the ark and mercy seat, "the ark of the testimony, and the mercy seat that is thereupon" — the holiest things, where God met his people; the witnesses note that all was to be made "according to the pattern showed to Moses," nothing of man's devising; the witnesses note the meaning, that the holy things, each with its meaning and type of Christ, were now to be wrought by the gifted men; the witnesses note that God's house and all its holy things are made by his own appointment and gifting; so that the tabernacle, ark, and mercy seat are to be made; and all the furniture of the tabernacle. The witnesses note The tabernacle of the congregation, and the ark of the testimony, and the mercy seat that is thereupon. The witnesses note and all the furniture of the tabernacle.

The witnesses note the tabernacle, the ark of the testimony, the mercy seat, and all the furniture named among the things the gifted workmen should make — all to be wrought after the pattern showed to Moses, nothing of man's devising; God's house and all its holy things made by his own appointment and gifting.

So the witnesses make the verse the beginning of the list of holy things to be made. The lesson the expositors draw is that the tabernacle, ark, and mercy seat were to be made after the pattern, by the gifted workmen, all by God's appointment. The witnesses note the the tabernacle: "the tabernacle of the congregation" — Gill notes God's dwelling; the the tabernacle; the God's dwelling; the after the pattern; the the chief; the made. The expositors observe the ark and mercy seat: "the ark... and the mercy seat" — the holiest things; the ark and mercy seat; the the holiest; the the testimony; the God met; the his people. The witnesses press the after the pattern: "according to the pattern" — nothing of man; the after the pattern; the nothing of man; the showed to Moses; the not devised; the by rule. The expositors draw the lesson on the each a type: each holy thing a type; the each a type; the each; the a type; the of Christ; the its meaning. The expositors note the by his appointment: by God's own appointment and gifting. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and all the furniture of the tabernacle, and the holy things. The verse thus records the holy things to be made — teaching that the tabernacle, ark, and mercy seat were wrought after the pattern, by the gifted workmen.
8:
"And the table and his furniture, and the pure candlestick with all his furniture, and the altar of incense," The witnesses note the table, the pure candlestick, and the altar of incense among the things to be made. Adam Clarke notes the pure candlestick was so called either from the pure gold of which it was made, or the brightness and splendour of its workmanship, or the light which it gave. The witnesses note God naming the table with its furniture, the pure candlestick with its furniture, and the altar of incense, to be made; the furniture of the holy place to be wrought by the gifted men — that the table, light, and incense-altar are to be made: "the table and his furniture, and the pure candlestick with all his furniture, and the altar of incense"; the witnesses note the table, "the table and his furniture" — the shewbread table, "with its dishes, spoons, and bowls"; the witnesses note the candlestick, "the pure candlestick with all his furniture" — so called "from the pure gold of which it was made," or "the brightness... of its workmanship," or "the light which it gave," with its lamps and snuffers; the witnesses note the incense-altar, "the altar of incense" — the golden altar where the sweet incense was burned; the witnesses note that these three, the table, the candlestick, and the altar of incense, were the furniture of the holy place; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the shewbread (Christ the bread of life), the candlestick (Christ the light), and the incense-altar (the prayers of the saints and Christ's intercession) were each a type; the witnesses note that all the holy furniture was to be wrought by the gifted men; so that the table, candlestick, and incense-altar are to be made; and the altar of incense. The witnesses note And the table and his furniture, and the pure candlestick with all his furniture. The witnesses note and the altar of incense.

The witnesses note the table, the pure candlestick (so called from its pure gold and the light it gave), and the altar of incense named among the furniture of the holy place — each a type the commentators draw out: the shewbread (Christ the bread of life), the candlestick (Christ the light), and the incense-altar (the prayers of the saints and Christ's intercession).

So the witnesses make the verse the furniture of the holy place to be made. The lesson the expositors draw is that the table, candlestick, and altar of incense were to be wrought by the gifted men, each a type of Christ. The witnesses note the the table: "the table and his furniture" — Gill notes the shewbread; the the table; the the shewbread; the the dishes; the the spoons; the the bowls. The expositors observe the the pure candlestick: "the pure candlestick" — Clarke notes pure gold; the the pure candlestick; the pure gold; the the brightness; the the light; the the lamps. The witnesses press the altar of incense: "the altar of incense" — the golden altar; the altar of incense; the the golden altar; the the incense; the burned; the sweet. The expositors draw the lesson on the each a type: each a type of Christ; the each a type; the each; the a type; the of Christ; the the bread and light. The expositors note the wrought by the gifted: wrought by the gifted men. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and the altar of incense, and the furniture. The verse thus records the furniture of the holy place — teaching that the table, candlestick, and incense-altar were to be made, each a type of Christ.
9:
"And the altar of burnt offering with all his furniture, and the laver and his foot," The witnesses note the altar of burnt offering and the laver among the things to be made. Adam Clarke notes the altar of burnt offering, and the laver and its foot, the pedestal on which it stood. The witnesses note God naming the altar of burnt offering with its furniture, and the laver and its foot, to be made; the vessels of the court to be wrought by the gifted men — that the altar and laver are to be made: "the altar of burnt offering with all his furniture, and the laver and his foot"; the witnesses note the altar, "the altar of burnt offering with all his furniture" — the brazen altar, "made of shittim wood covered with brass," with "its pans, shovels, basins," and the rest of its vessels; the witnesses note the laver, "the laver and his foot" — the brazen laver, with "the pedestal on which it stood," where the priests washed; the witnesses note that these were the vessels of the court, the altar of sacrifice and the laver of washing, the first things met on entering; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the brazen altar (the cross of Christ, where sacrifice is made) and the laver (the washing of regeneration and the word) were each a type, the way to God being through sacrifice and cleansing; the witnesses note that all the vessels, of court and sanctuary, were to be made by the gifted men; so that the altar and laver are to be made; and the laver and his foot. The witnesses note And the altar of burnt offering with all his furniture. The witnesses note and the laver and his foot.

The witnesses note the brazen altar of burnt offering with its furniture and the laver and its foot named among the vessels of the court — the altar of sacrifice and the laver of washing; each a type, the brazen altar (the cross of Christ, where sacrifice is made) and the laver (the washing of regeneration and the word), the way to God through sacrifice and cleansing.

So the witnesses make the verse the vessels of the court to be made. The lesson the expositors draw is that the altar of burnt offering and the laver were to be wrought, each a type, the way to God through sacrifice and cleansing. The witnesses note the the altar: "the altar of burnt offering" — Clarke notes brass; the the altar; the brass; the shittim wood; the the pans; the the shovels. The expositors observe the the laver: "the laver and his foot" — the pedestal; the the laver; the the pedestal; the brazen; the the priests; the washed. The witnesses press the the court vessels: the vessels of the court; the the court vessels; the the court; the the altar; the the laver; the first met. The expositors draw the lesson on the the cross of Christ: the brazen altar, the cross; the the cross of Christ; the the cross; the Christ; the sacrifice; the made. The witnesses observe the the washing: the laver, the washing; the the washing; the the laver; the regeneration; the the word; the cleansing. The expositors note the sacrifice and cleansing: the way through sacrifice and cleansing; the sacrifice and cleansing; the sacrifice; the cleansing; the the way; the to God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And the altar of burnt offering with all his furniture, the and the laver and his foot, and the vessels. The verse thus records the vessels of the court — teaching that the altar and laver were to be made, each a type, the way to God through sacrifice and cleansing.
10:
"And the cloths of service, and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons, to minister in the priest's office," The witnesses note the cloths of service and the holy garments of the priests among the things to be made. Gill notes the cloths of service were either those the priests ministered in during the time of service, and never wore but when in it, or the cloths of blue and purple in which the holy things were wrapped. The witnesses note God naming the cloths of service and the holy garments of Aaron and his sons, to minister in the priest's office, to be made; the priestly vestments and service-cloths to be wrought by the gifted men — that the garments of the priests are to be made: "the cloths of service, and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons, to minister in the priest's office"; the witnesses note the cloths of service, that these were "either those the priests ministered in" only "in the time of service," or "cloths of blue and purple" in which the holy vessels were wrapped for carriage; the witnesses note the holy garments, "the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons" — the high priest's glorious vestments and the plainer garments of the common priests, all "to minister in the priest's office"; the witnesses note that the garments too, like the furniture, were to be wrought by the gifted men; the witnesses note the type, that the holy garments shadow the righteousness and graces with which Christ and his people are clothed for God's service; the witnesses note that all that pertains to God's service, even the garments, is made by his appointment; so that the service-cloths and holy garments are to be made; to minister in the priest's office. The witnesses note And the cloths of service, and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons. The witnesses note to minister in the priest's office.

The witnesses note the cloths of service and the holy garments of Aaron and his sons named among the things to be made — the priestly vestments to minister in the priest's office; a type of the righteousness and graces with which Christ and his people are clothed for God's service, all that pertains to his service made by his appointment.

So the witnesses make the verse the priestly garments to be made. The lesson the expositors draw is that the service-cloths and holy garments were to be wrought by the gifted men, a type of being clothed with righteousness for God's service. The witnesses note the cloths of service: "the cloths of service" — Gill notes for the time of service; the cloths of service; the for the time of service; the or blue and purple; the wrapped; the the vessels. 11:
"And the anointing oil, and sweet incense for the holy place: according to all that I have commanded thee shall they do." The witnesses note the anointing oil and sweet incense to be made, all according to God's command. Adam Clarke notes the anointing oil and the sweet incense, of the composition of both which directions had been given. The witnesses note God naming the anointing oil and the sweet incense for the holy place, to be made, with the charge that all be done according to all he had commanded; the last of the holy things and the seal of the whole, that all be made after the pattern — that the oil and incense complete the list, and all is by command: "the anointing oil, and sweet incense for the holy place: according to all that I have commanded thee shall they do"; the witnesses note the oil and incense, "the anointing oil, and sweet incense for the holy place" — the holy oil of consecration and the sweet incense, each "of [a special] composition" appointed by God (ch. 30); the witnesses note the type, that the anointing oil shadows the Spirit and the sweet incense the prayers of the saints and Christ's intercession, a sweet savour before God; the witnesses note the great charge, "according to all that I have commanded thee shall they do" — that all, furniture and garments and oil and incense, was to be made exactly "according to all" God commanded, nothing of man's devising; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that in God's service all is to be done by his rule and command, not by man's invention; the witnesses note that God will be served only his own way; so that the oil and incense are made, and all by command; according to all that I have commanded thee shall they do. The witnesses note And the anointing oil, and sweet incense for the holy place. The witnesses note according to all that I have commanded thee shall they do.

The witnesses note the anointing oil and sweet incense named last, with the charge that all be made according to all God commanded — the oil a type of the Spirit, the incense of the prayers of the saints and Christ's intercession; in God's service all to be done by his rule and command, not by man's invention, for God will be served only his own way.

So the witnesses make the verse the last of the holy things and the seal of the whole. The lesson the expositors draw is that the oil and incense completed the list, and all was to be made according to all God commanded, nothing of man's devising. The witnesses note the oil and incense: "the anointing oil, and sweet incense" — Clarke notes a special composition; the oil and incense; the a special composition; the the holy oil; the the sweet incense; the appointed. The expositors note the the seal: the seal of the whole, all made after the pattern. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the according to all that I have commanded thee shall they do, and the command. The verse thus records the last holy things and the seal — teaching that the oil and incense completed the list, all to be made according to all God commanded.
12:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the LORD speaking again, now to repeat the law of the sabbath. Gill notes that, after describing the place of worship, appointing the priests, and ordering all things and the workmen, God repeats the law of the sabbath and reminds of the time of worship. The witnesses note God speaking again to Moses, now to repeat the sabbath law after all the orders about the tabernacle; the turning from the place and work of worship to the sabbath, the time of worship — that, having ordered the tabernacle, God now guards the sabbath: "the LORD spake unto Moses, saying"; the witnesses note the connexion, that this came "after he had described... the place of worship... appointed the priests... ordered the making of all things... and the workmen"; the witnesses note that now God "repeats the law of the sabbath, and puts in mind of the time of worship," having ordered the place; the witnesses note the great meaning the commentators draw, that even the holy work of building the tabernacle must not break the sabbath, the time of rest being as sacred as the place of worship; the witnesses note that God will have his sabbath kept even amid his own work; the witnesses note that the order of place and time in worship is of God's appointing; so that God speaks again to guard the sabbath; the LORD spake unto Moses, saying. The witnesses note And the LORD spake unto Moses. The witnesses note saying.

The witnesses note the LORD speaking again to repeat the sabbath law after all the orders about the tabernacle — turning from the place and work of worship to the sabbath, the time of worship; even the holy work of building the tabernacle must not break the sabbath, the time of rest as sacred as the place of worship.

So the witnesses make the verse the turning from the place of worship to the sabbath. The lesson the expositors draw is that God, having ordered the tabernacle, now guards the sabbath, even his own holy work not to break it. The witnesses note the spake again: "the LORD spake unto Moses" — Gill notes after the orders; the spake again; the after the orders; the the place; the the priests; the the workmen. The expositors observe the repeats the sabbath: now repeats the sabbath law; the repeats the sabbath; the now; the the sabbath; the the law; the the time. The witnesses press the the time of worship: the time of worship; the the time; the of worship; the the sabbath; the as sacred; the the place. The expositors draw the lesson on the not break it: even his work not to break it; the not break it; the even his work; the not to break; the the sabbath; the the rest. The witnesses observe the kept amid his work: the sabbath kept amid his work; the kept amid his work; the kept; the amid; the his work; the the rest. The expositors note the place and time: the order of place and time; the place and time; the place; the time; the of worship; the appointed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And the LORD spake unto Moses, the saying, and the sabbath. The verse thus records the turning to the sabbath — teaching that God, having ordered the tabernacle, now guards the sabbath, even his own work not to break it.
13:
"Speak thou also unto the children of Israel, saying, Verily my sabbaths ye shall keep: for it is a sign between me and you throughout your generations; that ye may know that I am the LORD that doth sanctify you." The witnesses note God charging Israel to keep his sabbaths as a sign that he sanctifies them. Gill notes that, notwithstanding all that was ordered about making the tabernacle, this was not to be understood to the violation of the sabbath, in which no work was to be done, not even any relating to the tabernacle. The witnesses note God charging Israel to keep his sabbaths, for it was a sign between him and them throughout their generations, that they might know he is the LORD who sanctifies them; the sabbath as a sign of God's sanctifying his people — that the sabbath marks them as the LORD's, whom he sanctifies: "Verily my sabbaths ye shall keep: for it is a sign between me and you... that ye may know that I am the LORD that doth sanctify you"; the witnesses note the charge, "my sabbaths ye shall keep" — that, even amid the work of the tabernacle, "no work was to be done... not even any relating to the tabernacle" on the sabbath; the witnesses note the great end, "that ye may know that I am the LORD that doth sanctify you" — that the keeping of the sabbath, set apart to God, witnessed that he had set apart and sanctified them as his own; the witnesses note the meaning, that as God sanctified the day, so he sanctifies his people, the rest a sign of the holiness he works in them; the witnesses note the type, that the sabbath shadows the rest and sanctification God's people have in Christ; so that the sabbath is a sign that the LORD sanctifies them; that ye may know that I am the LORD that doth sanctify you. The witnesses note Verily my sabbaths ye shall keep: for it is a sign between me and you throughout your generations. The witnesses note that ye may know that I am the LORD that doth sanctify you.

The witnesses note God charging Israel to keep his sabbaths — even no tabernacle-work on the sabbath — as a sign between him and them, that they might know he is the LORD who sanctifies them; as God sanctified the day, so he sanctifies his people, the rest a sign of the holiness he works in them, shadowing the rest his people have in Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the sabbath as a sign of God's sanctifying his people. The lesson the expositors draw is that the sabbath, kept holy to God, was a sign that he had set apart and sanctified Israel as his own, shadowing the rest in Christ. The witnesses note the my sabbaths: "my sabbaths ye shall keep" — Gill notes no work, not even the tabernacle's; the my sabbaths; the no work; the not the tabernacle; the kept; the holy. The expositors observe the a sign: "a sign between me and you" — a token of the covenant; the a sign; the a token; the the covenant; the their generations; the between. The witnesses press the I sanctify you: "the LORD that doth sanctify you" — set apart; the his own. The expositors draw the lesson on the sanctifies the day and them: as the day, so the people. The expositors note the marks them his: the sabbath marks them his. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that ye may know that I am the LORD that doth sanctify you, and the sign. The verse thus records the sabbath as a sign — teaching that it witnessed God had sanctified Israel as his own, shadowing the rest in Christ.
14:
"Ye shall keep the sabbath therefore; for it is holy unto you: every one that defileth it shall surely be put to death: for whosoever doeth any work therein, that soul shall be cut off from among his people." The witnesses note the charge to keep the sabbath holy, on pain of death for defiling it. Adam Clarke notes that to defile the sabbath is by any kind of idolatrous or profane worship, and the magistrates were to examine the business and put the guilty to death. The witnesses note God charging Israel to keep the sabbath, for it was holy, with death the penalty for defiling it or working on it; the strictness of the sabbath law and its solemn sanction — that the sabbath must be kept holy on pain of death: "Ye shall keep the sabbath therefore; for it is holy unto you: every one that defileth it shall surely be put to death"; the witnesses note the charge, "keep the sabbath... for it is holy unto you" — strictly to observe it, "according to the rules given," as a holy day; the witnesses note the sanction, "every one that defileth it shall surely be put to death" — that to "defile" the sabbath "by any kind of idolatrous or profane" act, or to do work in it, was a capital crime, the "magistrates" to try and execute the guilty; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the solemn severity marks how high God values the keeping of his holy day and his worship; the witnesses note that the breach of God's holy things is no light matter; the witnesses note that holiness is to be kept on pain of his judgment; so that the sabbath is kept holy on pain of death; that soul shall be cut off from among his people. The witnesses note Ye shall keep the sabbath therefore; for it is holy unto you: every one that defileth it shall surely be put to death. The witnesses note for whosoever doeth any work therein, that soul shall be cut off from among his people.

The witnesses note God charging Israel to keep the sabbath holy, with death for defiling it (by profane or idolatrous act) or working on it, the offender cut off from his people — the solemn severity marking how high God values the keeping of his holy day and worship, the breach of his holy things no light matter.

So the witnesses make the verse the strictness of the sabbath law and its solemn sanction. The lesson the expositors draw is that the sabbath was to be kept holy on pain of death, marking how high God values his holy day, the breach of his holy things no light matter. The witnesses note the keep it holy: "keep the sabbath... for it is holy" — Clarke notes strictly; the keep it holy; the strictly; the the rules; the a holy day; the observe. The expositors observe the defileth it: "every one that defileth it" — by profane act; the defileth it; the by profane act; the idolatrous; the the magistrates; the tried. The witnesses press the put to death: "surely be put to death" — capital; the the guilty. The expositors draw the lesson on the cut off: "that soul shall be cut off" — rooted out; the cut off; the rooted out; the destroyed; the from Israel; the his people. The expositors note the no light matter: the breach of his holy things no light matter. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for whosoever doeth any work therein that soul shall be cut off from among his people, and the sabbath. The verse thus records the strictness of the sabbath — teaching that it was kept holy on pain of death, the breach of God's holy things no light matter.
15:
"Six days may work be done; but in the seventh is the sabbath of rest, holy to the LORD: whosoever doeth any work in the sabbath day, he shall surely be put to death." The witnesses note the six days for work and the seventh as the holy sabbath of rest. Gill notes that six days work may be done, allowed an Israelite if he would, this being not a command to work but a permission; and seeing they had so many days, it was not unreasonable that God should claim one in seven. The witnesses note God allowing work the six days but appointing the seventh the sabbath of rest, holy to him, on pain of death for working in it; the granting of the six days and the claiming of the seventh — that God gives six and claims one: "Six days may work be done; but in the seventh is the sabbath of rest, holy to the LORD"; the witnesses note the grant, "Six days may work be done" — that this is "not a command to work, but a permission," the six days "allowed... if he would"; the witnesses note the seventh, "in the seventh is the sabbath of rest, holy to the LORD" — a day of rest, set apart and holy to God; the witnesses note the sanction, "whosoever doeth any work in the sabbath day... shall surely be put to death" — the same solemn penalty repeated; the witnesses note the meaning, that God deals bountifully, giving six and asking but one, and that one for our rest and his worship; the witnesses note that it is just and reasonable to give God his day; so that six days are for work, the seventh holy to God; he shall surely be put to death. The witnesses note Six days may work be done; but in the seventh is the sabbath of rest, holy to the LORD. The witnesses note whosoever doeth any work in the sabbath day, he shall surely be put to death.

The witnesses note God allowing work the six days (a permission, not a command) but claiming the seventh as the sabbath of rest, holy to him, on pain of death — God dealing bountifully, giving six days and asking but one, and that one for our rest and his worship; it is just and reasonable to give God his day.

So the witnesses make the verse the granting of the six days and the claiming of the seventh. The lesson the expositors draw is that God gives six days for work and claims the seventh as holy rest, dealing bountifully, it being just to give God his one day. The witnesses note the six days work: "Six days may work be done" — Gill notes a permission; the six days work; the a permission; the not a command; the allowed; the if he would. The expositors observe the claims one in seven: God claims one in seven; the his own. The witnesses press the the sabbath of rest: "the sabbath of rest, holy to the LORD" — set apart; the the sabbath of rest; the set apart; the holy; the to God; the the seventh. The expositors draw the lesson on the deals bountifully: God deals bountifully; the deals bountifully; the deals; the bountifully; the gives six; the asks one. The expositors note the just and reasonable: just to give God his day. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whosoever doeth any work in the sabbath day he shall surely be put to death, and the rest. The verse thus records the six days and the seventh — teaching that God gives six and claims one, dealing bountifully, it being just to give God his day.
16:
"Wherefore the children of Israel shall keep the sabbath, to observe the sabbath throughout their generations, for a perpetual covenant." The witnesses note Israel to keep the sabbath throughout their generations for a perpetual covenant. Adam Clarke notes it is called a perpetual covenant because it is a sign of that future rest and blessedness which remains for the people of God. The witnesses note God appointing Israel to keep the sabbath through all their generations as a perpetual covenant; the lasting obligation of the sabbath and its covenant meaning — that the sabbath is a standing covenant-sign: "the children of Israel shall keep the sabbath, to observe the sabbath throughout their generations, for a perpetual covenant"; the witnesses note the duty, "shall keep the sabbath... throughout their generations" — that the sabbath was binding on Israel in every age; the witnesses note the covenant, "for a perpetual covenant" — that it was a lasting sign of the covenant between God and his people; the witnesses note the meaning Clarke draws, that it is "a perpetual covenant" because "it is a sign of that future rest and blessedness which remains for the people of God" (Heb. 4:9); the witnesses note that Gill marks the seventh-day sabbath as "ceremonial and shadowy," a shadow of the rest to come, the substance being Christ; the witnesses note that the sabbath points beyond itself to the everlasting rest in God; the witnesses note that God's people are bound to honour his rest, which shadows the rest he gives; so that Israel keeps the sabbath, a perpetual covenant-sign; for a perpetual covenant. The witnesses note Wherefore the children of Israel shall keep the sabbath, to observe the sabbath throughout their generations. The witnesses note for a perpetual covenant.

The witnesses note Israel bound to keep the sabbath through all generations as a perpetual covenant — a lasting sign of the covenant, and (as Clarke draws) a sign of that future rest and blessedness which remains for the people of God; the seventh-day sabbath ceremonial and shadowy, pointing beyond itself to the everlasting rest in God, the substance being Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the lasting obligation of the sabbath and its covenant meaning. The lesson the expositors draw is that the sabbath was a perpetual covenant-sign, pointing to the future rest that remains for God's people, the substance Christ. The witnesses note the keep throughout generations: "keep the sabbath... throughout their generations" — Clarke notes binding in every age; the keep throughout generations; the binding; the every age; the the duty; the observe. The expositors observe the perpetual covenant: "for a perpetual covenant" — a lasting sign; the perpetual covenant; the a lasting sign; the the covenant; the between God; the his people. The witnesses press the future rest: "a sign of... future rest" — that remains; the future rest; the that remains; the blessedness; the for God's people; the to come. The expositors draw the lesson on the ceremonial and shadowy: the sabbath ceremonial and shadowy; the ceremonial and shadowy; the ceremonial; the shadowy; the a shadow; the the rest. The witnesses observe the substance Christ: the substance being Christ; the substance Christ; the the substance; the Christ; the the rest; the in him. The expositors note the points beyond itself: points beyond itself; the points beyond itself; the points; the beyond; the itself; the the everlasting rest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Wherefore the children of Israel shall keep the sabbath to observe the sabbath throughout their generations, the for a perpetual covenant, and the covenant. The verse thus records the perpetual covenant — teaching that the sabbath pointed to the future rest that remains for God's people, the substance Christ.
17:
"It is a sign between me and the children of Israel for ever: for in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, and on the seventh day he rested, and was refreshed." The witnesses note the sabbath a sign for ever, grounded in God's resting on the seventh day of creation. Gill notes it is a sign for ever in like sense as the land of Canaan was given for an everlasting possession, and circumcision and the passover were for ever, that is, unto the end of the Jewish state. The witnesses note God declaring the sabbath a sign between him and Israel for ever, grounded in his making heaven and earth in six days and resting the seventh; the perpetual sign and its ground in creation — that the sabbath rests on God's own resting: "It is a sign between me and the children of Israel for ever: for in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, and on the seventh day he rested, and was refreshed"; the witnesses note the sign, "a sign between me and the children of Israel for ever" — "for ever" in the sense that the land, circumcision, and the passover were "for ever," that is, "unto the end of the Jewish... state," the ceremonial sabbath lasting till Christ; the witnesses note the ground, "in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, and on the seventh day he rested" — that the weekly rest was founded on God's own resting after the six days of creation; the witnesses note the meaning, that man's rest follows God's, and that the sabbath roots in creation itself; the witnesses note the type, that God's rest, and the sabbath, point to the rest that remains for his people in Christ; so that the sabbath is a sign for ever, grounded in creation; and on the seventh day he rested, and was refreshed. The witnesses note It is a sign between me and the children of Israel for ever: for in six days the LORD made heaven and earth. The witnesses note and on the seventh day he rested, and was refreshed.

The witnesses note the sabbath a sign between God and Israel, grounded in his making heaven and earth in six days and resting the seventh, when he "was refreshed" (spoken after the manner of men, God taking delight in his finished work) — man's rest following God's, the sabbath rooting in creation itself and pointing to the rest that remains for his people in Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the perpetual sign and its ground in creation. The lesson the expositors draw is that the sabbath rests on God's own resting after the six days of creation, a sign pointing to the rest that remains for his people. The witnesses note the a sign for ever: "a sign... for ever" — Gill notes as the land and passover; the a sign for ever; the as the land; the the passover; the unto the end; the the Jewish state. The expositors observe the made in six days: "in six days the LORD made heaven and earth" — creation; the made in six days; the creation; the the six days; the heaven; the earth. The witnesses press the he rested: "on the seventh day he rested" — God's own rest; the the ground. The expositors draw the lesson on the was refreshed: "and was refreshed" — after the manner of men, God taking delight in his work. The expositors note the rest in Christ: points to the rest that remains in Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and on the seventh day he rested and was refreshed, and the sign. The verse thus records the sign grounded in creation — teaching that the sabbath rests on God's own resting, pointing to the rest that remains for his people.
18:
"And he gave unto Moses, when he had made an end of communing with him upon mount Sinai, two tables of testimony, tables of stone, written with the finger of God." The witnesses note the two tables of testimony given to Moses, written with the finger of God. Gill notes that, after all those laws and instructions, God ceased to converse with Moses in that manner, and at parting gave him two tables of testimony, the two tables of the law. The witnesses note God giving Moses, when their communing ended, the two tables of testimony, tables of stone written with his own finger; the close of the communing and the gift of the law in stone — that God seals all by giving the two tables written by himself: "he gave unto Moses, when he had made an end of communing with him upon mount Sinai, two tables of testimony, tables of stone, written with the finger of God"; the witnesses note the close, "when he had made an end of communing with him" — that, after "all those laws, orders, and instructions," God "ceased to converse with him" in that manner; the witnesses note the gift, "two tables of testimony, tables of stone" — the two tables of the law (the Ten Commandments), called "the testimony," graven on stone; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw, that the moral law, being God's own writing, is of his own authority, abiding and sure, the rule of his people; the witnesses note that the law on stone shadows the law to be written by the Spirit on the heart (2 Cor. 3:3; Jer. 31:33); the witnesses note that God himself is the author of his law; so that Moses receives the two tables written by God; written with the finger of God. The witnesses note he gave unto Moses, when he had made an end of communing with him upon mount Sinai, two tables of testimony, tables of stone. The witnesses note written with the finger of God.

The witnesses note God giving Moses, at the close of their communing, the two tables of testimony — the law graven on stone, "written with the finger of God," by his own immediate hand; the moral law, being God's own writing, of his own authority, abiding and sure, the law on stone shadowing the law to be written by the Spirit on the heart.

So the witnesses make the verse the close of the communing and the gift of the law in stone. The lesson the expositors draw is that God gave the two tables written with his own finger, the law of his own authority, shadowing the law written by the Spirit on the heart. The witnesses note the made an end: "made an end of communing" — Gill notes ceased to converse; the made an end; the ceased to converse; the all the laws; the the instructions; the at parting. The expositors observe the two tables: "two tables of testimony, tables of stone" — the law; the two tables; the the law; the the testimony; the the commandments; the on stone. The witnesses press the finger of God: "written with the finger of God" — his own hand; the not man. The expositors draw the lesson on the his own authority: the law of his own authority; the his own authority; the his own; the authority; the abiding; the sure. The expositors note the God the author: God the author of his law. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the written with the finger of God, and the tables. The verse thus records the gift of the law in stone — teaching that God gave the two tables written by his own finger, shadowing the law written by the Spirit on the heart.
